1:
On this verse the cloud of witnesses is, at root, unanimous: across the Greek and Latin fathers, the Reformers, and the modern grammatical commentators alike, John 1:1 is read as the church's charter for the eternal deity of Christ — the Word is eternal (not made), personal and distinct from the Father ("with God"), and himself fully divine ("was God"). No school dissents from this; their differences are of emphasis and approach, not of doctrine.

The grammatical-historical commentators are the most active here, and supply the verse's sharpest exegesis. With one voice they hear the opening as a deliberate echo of Genesis 1:1 — a Jew would name Genesis itself by its first word, Bĕrēshîth — so that the Word by whom creation was made now brings about redemption. They press the tenses: John writes the timeless "was" (ēn), not "became," carrying thought (as Robertson puts it) beyond the beginning of creation into eternity. And they handle the grammar a later age would seize on: "the Word was God" lacks the article before God not to weaken it to "a god" but to mark it as a predicate of nature — the Word is fully divine, while remaining the distinct One who was "with God."

The Reformed voices (Calvin, Gill) read doctrinally: this is the essential, personal Word — not the written word — whose eternal divinity grounds the very necessity of redemption through the Son. The Latin fathers, with Augustine, strike a pastoral and epistemic note: "the natural man perceiveth not the things of the Spirit," so the height of the verse is reached by faith, not cleverness — Augustine recalls the Platonist who said these words deserved to be written in letters of gold. The Antiochene Chrysostom frames our very approach: we need not trace John's family or schooling, "since it is not he, but God by him, that speaks to mankind."

Where the witnesses press differently is the sense of "beginning." Most (Lightfoot, Wesley, and the expositors) anchor it in the beginning of creation in Genesis; the grammarians and the Alexandrian Origen — here the outlier — reach past creation: Origen catalogues the word's many scriptural senses and finds Christ to be himself the Beginning as Wisdom. But this is a deepening, not a disagreement: every school confesses the same eternal, divine, personal Word who is the doorway to all the Gospel will unfold.
14:
If verse 1 guards the Word's eternal deity, the witnesses hear verse 14 as its astonishing counterpart, and again they are unanimous on the substance: the same eternal Word truly "became flesh" — took full and real humanity — without ceasing to be God. The schools converge especially against any docetic dilution: this is a real incarnation, not an appearance.

The grammatical-historical commentators again do the close work. They mark the decisive shift of verb — John now writes egeneto ("became"), the historic event of the Incarnation, over against the timeless ēn of verse 1 (Robertson) — and note that "flesh" stands without the article, so the clause can never be reversed into flesh dissolving the Word. On the word "flesh" they (with Barnes) settle its sense as full human nature, the whole man; Ellicott draws the line explicitly against the Docetists who denied a real body: "Man came to be a son of God, because the Son of God became man."

The Reformed voices (Calvin, Gill) add Trinitarian precision and continuity: it was the Son alone — not the Father or the Spirit — who was clothed with our flesh and shown openly to the world; "that very person who was in the beginning." The Antiochene Chrysostom presses the logic of grace: we are made sons of God only because the Word was made flesh, and we could never have seen his glory had he not, by union with our humanity, become visible to us.

On "we beheld his glory," the Wesleyan reading (Wesley) is the most concrete: the "we" are the apostles — Peter, James, and John, who saw the glory "as of the only begotten" — and Wesley canvasses the senses of "flesh" (corrupt nature, the body, or, here, the whole man) before settling, with the consensus, on full humanity. He turns the verse Godward: against our native condition as "liars and children of wrath," grace and truth come in him. There is no real dissent here, only complementary angles on one confession: the eternal Word truly took our whole humanity, tabernacled among us, and let his divine glory be seen — that we might receive of his fulness, grace upon grace.
